**Mark Joseph Carney** (born March 16, 1965) is a Canadian politician and economist who has served as the 24th [prime minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") since 2025. He has also been [leader of the Liberal Party](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Leader of the Liberal Party of Canada") and the [member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Canada) "Member of Parliament (Canada)") (MP) for [Nepean](/wiki/Nepean_(federal_electoral_district) "Nepean (federal electoral district)") since 2025. He previously was [Governor of the Bank of Canada](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_Canada "Governor of the Bank of Canada") from 2008 to 2013 and [Governor of the Bank of England](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_England "Governor of the Bank of England") from 2013 to 2020.

Carney was born in [Fort Smith](/wiki/Fort_Smith,_Northwest_Territories "Fort Smith, Northwest Territories"), Northwest Territories, and raised in [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), Alberta. He graduated with a bachelor's degree in economics from [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") in 1987, and earned a master's degree in 1993 and a doctorate in 1995 from the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), both in economics. Carney worked at [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") before joining the [Bank of Canada](/wiki/Bank_of_Canada "Bank of Canada") in 2003 as a deputy governor. In 2004, he joined the [Department of Finance Canada](/wiki/Department_of_Finance_Canada "Department of Finance Canada") as a senior associate [deputy minister](/wiki/Deputy_minister_(Canada) "Deputy minister (Canada)"). Carney served as the eighth [governor of the Bank of Canada](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_Canada "Governor of the Bank of Canada") from 2008 to 2013. As governor, he oversaw Canadian [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") during the [2008 global financial crisis](/wiki/2008_global_financial_crisis "2008 global financial crisis"). He was appointed as the second chair of the [Financial Stability Board](/wiki/Financial_Stability_Board "Financial Stability Board") in 2011, serving for two terms until 2018. After his term as Governor of the Bank of Canada, Carney was appointed as the 120th [governor of the Bank of England](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_England "Governor of the Bank of England"), becoming the first non-British citizen to be appointed to the role. He served from 2013 to 2020, leading the central bank's responses to [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") and the early phase of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom").

Following his governorship, Carney held several roles in the private and public sectors. He was chair of [Bloomberg L.P.](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P."), vice-chair at [Brookfield Asset Management](/wiki/Brookfield_Asset_Management "Brookfield Asset Management")—a [spin-off](/wiki/Corporate_spin-off "Corporate spin-off") of [Brookfield Corporation](/wiki/Brookfield_Corporation "Brookfield Corporation")—and co-chair of the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank")'s private sector investment lab. From 2020, Carney was the [United Nations Secretary-General](/wiki/United_Nations_Secretary-General "United Nations Secretary-General")'s [Special Envoy](/wiki/Special_Envoy_of_the_Secretary-General "Special Envoy of the Secretary-General") on Climate Action and Finance. Carney was also an informal advisor to Canadian prime minister [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau") at the start of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Canada "COVID-19 pandemic in Canada"), before returning to the private sector. In 2024, he was appointed as chair of the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada")'s Task Force on Economic Growth. In January 2025, after Trudeau announced [his resignation](/wiki/Resignation_of_Justin_Trudeau "Resignation of Justin Trudeau") amid [a political crisis](/wiki/2024%E2%80%932025_Canadian_political_crisis "2024–2025 Canadian political crisis"), Carney entered the [Liberal Party leadership election](/wiki/2025_Liberal_Party_of_Canada_leadership_election "2025 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election"), winning a landslide victory that March. After he became party leader, Carney was appointed prime minister, becoming the first [prime minister in Canadian history](/wiki/List_of_prime_ministers_of_Canada "List of prime ministers of Canada") never to have held elected office. Carney then advised the [governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") to [dissolve Parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") and trigger [a federal election](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_election "2025 Canadian federal election"). He led the Liberals to a [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government")—overturning [earlier poor opinion polling](/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2025_Canadian_federal_election#Pre-campaign_period "Opinion polling for the 2025 Canadian federal election") to win the party's fourth consecutive mandate since [2015](/wiki/2015_Canadian_federal_election "2015 Canadian federal election")—and was elected for the first time to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") in the [riding](/wiki/Electoral_district_(Canada) "Electoral district (Canada)") of [Nepean](/wiki/Nepean_(federal_electoral_district) "Nepean (federal electoral district)").

During [his tenure](/wiki/Premiership_of_Mark_Carney "Premiership of Mark Carney") as prime minister, Carney removed the federal [consumer carbon tax](/wiki/Carbon_pricing_in_Canada "Carbon pricing in Canada"), relaxed environmental regulations, enacted the *[One Canadian Economy Act](/wiki/One_Canadian_Economy_Act "One Canadian Economy Act")* to reduce interprovincial trade barriers and expedite [major infrastructure projects](/wiki/Major_Projects_Office "Major Projects Office") in response to the [ongoing trade war with the United States](/wiki/2025_United_States_trade_war_with_Canada_and_Mexico "2025 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico"), and launched the [Build Canada Homes](/wiki/Build_Canada_Homes "Build Canada Homes") agency. [Carney's government](/wiki/30th_Canadian_Ministry "30th Canadian Ministry") also announced a sharp increase in [defence spending](/wiki/Military_budget "Military budget"), [formally recognized](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Palestine "International recognition of Palestine") the [State of Palestine](/wiki/State_of_Palestine "State of Palestine"), oversaw an improvement in [relations with China](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93China_relations "Canada–China relations") and [with India](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93India_relations "Canada–India relations"), and has continued [support for Ukraine](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93Ukraine_relations#Response_to_the_Russo-Ukrainian_War "Canada–Ukraine relations") in the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)"). Carney is ideologically characterized as a [centrist](/wiki/Centrist "Centrist"), [technocrat](/wiki/Technocrat "Technocrat"), and a [Blue Grit](/wiki/Blue_Grit "Blue Grit") Liberal, and, as prime minister, has moved the Liberals towards the political centre.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Early life and education

Mark Joseph Carney was born on March 16, 1965, at St. Ann's General Hospital in [Fort Smith, Northwest Territories](/wiki/Fort_Smith,_Northwest_Territories "Fort Smith, Northwest Territories").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Scoffield-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) He has three siblings: an older brother and sister, Seán and Brenda, and a younger brother, Brian.[[6]](#cite_note-Scoffield-6) When Carney was six, his family moved to [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), Alberta's [Laurier Heights](/wiki/Laurier_Heights,_Edmonton "Laurier Heights, Edmonton") where he was raised.[[6]](#cite_note-Scoffield-6)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

He is the son of Verlie Margaret (née Kemper), a stay-at-home mother, and [Robert J. Carney](/wiki/Robert_J._Carney "Robert J. Carney"), a high school principal and Professor of Educational Foundations at the [University of Alberta](/wiki/University_of_Alberta "University of Alberta").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-RD01-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) His father was the Liberal candidate for [Edmonton South](/wiki/Edmonton_South "Edmonton South") in the [1980 federal election](/wiki/1980_Canadian_federal_election "1980 Canadian federal election"), placing second.[[13]](#cite_note-13) His mother returned to university to pursue a career in education when Carney was ten.[[10]](#cite_note-RD01-10) Three of his four grandparents were Irish, from [Aughagower](/wiki/Aughagower "Aughagower") in [County Mayo](/wiki/County_Mayo "County Mayo").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Carney graduated in the class of 1983 from [St. Francis Xavier High School](/wiki/St._Francis_Xavier_High_School_(Edmonton) "St. Francis Xavier High School (Edmonton)"), Edmonton, Alberta.[[16]](#cite_note-16) From there, he went on to study at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") with a partial scholarship and financial aid.[[6]](#cite_note-Scoffield-6)[[10]](#cite_note-RD01-10)[[17]](#cite_note-:2-17) During his Harvard years, he was backup [goalie](/wiki/Goaltender "Goaltender") for the [varsity ice hockey team](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_men%27s_ice_hockey "Harvard Crimson men's ice hockey")[[18]](#cite_note-18) and was a roommate of future NHL general manager [Peter Chiarelli](/wiki/Peter_Chiarelli_(ice_hockey) "Peter Chiarelli (ice hockey)") and former ice hockey player [Mark Benning](/wiki/Mark_Benning "Mark Benning").[[19]](#cite_note-:7-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) He lived at [Winthrop House](/wiki/Winthrop_House "Winthrop House"), and graduated in 1987 with a bachelor's degree in economics *magna cum laude*.[[21]](#cite_note-:8-21)

After Harvard, he travelled to Europe to study at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"). There he undertook [postgraduate studies](/wiki/Postgraduate_studies "Postgraduate studies") at [St Peter's College](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_College,_Oxford "St Peter's College, Oxford") and [Nuffield College](/wiki/Nuffield_College "Nuffield College"), where he received [Master of Philosophy](/wiki/Master_of_Philosophy "Master of Philosophy") (MPhil) and Doctor of Philosophy (DPhil) degrees in economics in 1993 and 1995, respectively.[[21]](#cite_note-:8-21)[[17]](#cite_note-:2-17)[[22]](#cite_note-22) His master's thesis was titled "Competitive advantage and the advantage of competition: a theoretical analysis of national champions, learning-by-doing and spillovers",[[23]](#cite_note-23) and his doctoral thesis was titled "The dynamic advantage of competition".[[24]](#cite_note-24) His [doctoral advisor](/wiki/Doctoral_advisor "Doctoral advisor") was [Margaret Meyer](/wiki/Margaret_A._Meyer "Margaret A. Meyer").[[25]](#cite_note-25) While at Oxford, he was co-captain of the [Oxford University Ice Hockey Club](/wiki/Oxford_University_Ice_Hockey_Club "Oxford University Ice Hockey Club") alongside fellow Canadian [David Lametti](/wiki/David_Lametti "David Lametti"), whom he would later appoint as [Permanent Representative of Canada to the UN](/wiki/Permanent_Representative_of_Canada_to_the_United_Nations "Permanent Representative of Canada to the United Nations").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 2021, he was elected to [Harvard University's Board of Overseers](/wiki/Harvard_Board_of_Overseers "Harvard Board of Overseers") through to 2027. He resigned in early 2025, around the time he assumed leadership of the Liberal Party.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Financial career

Carney spent thirteen years at [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs")[[30]](#cite_note-30) and worked in their [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), London, New York City, Tokyo, and [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") offices.[[31]](#cite_note-31) His progressively more senior positions included co-head of [sovereign risk](/wiki/Sovereign_risk "Sovereign risk"), executive director for emerging debt [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market"), and managing director for [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking"). He worked on South Africa's [post-apartheid](/wiki/Post-apartheid "Post-apartheid") venture into international [bond markets](/wiki/Bond_market "Bond market"), and was involved in Goldman's work with the [1998 Russian financial crisis](/wiki/1998_Russian_financial_crisis "1998 Russian financial crisis").[[6]](#cite_note-Scoffield-6)

In 2003, Carney left Goldman Sachs to join the [Bank of Canada](/wiki/Bank_of_Canada "Bank of Canada") as a deputy governor.[[32]](#cite_note-32) One year later, he was recruited to the [Department of Finance Canada](/wiki/Department_of_Finance_Canada "Department of Finance Canada") as [senior associate deputy minister](/wiki/Deputy_minister_(Canada) "Deputy minister (Canada)"), beginning on November 15, 2004.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

From November 2004 to October 2007, Carney was the senior associate deputy minister and [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") deputy in the [Department of Finance Canada](/wiki/Department_of_Finance_Canada "Department of Finance Canada"). He served under two [finance ministers](/wiki/Minister_of_Finance_(Canada) "Minister of Finance (Canada)"): [Ralph Goodale](/wiki/Ralph_Goodale "Ralph Goodale"), a [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada"); and [Jim Flaherty](/wiki/Jim_Flaherty "Jim Flaherty"), a [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada"). During this time, Carney oversaw the [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada")'s controversial plan to tax [income trusts](/wiki/Income_trust#The_Conservatives_propose_new_rules_for_income_trusts "Income trust") at source.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Carney was also the lead on the federal government's profitable sale of its 19 per cent stake in [Petro-Canada](/wiki/Petro-Canada "Petro-Canada").[[19]](#cite_note-:7-19)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Governor of the Bank of Canada (2008–2013)

In October 2007, Carney was appointed [Governor of the Bank of Canada](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_Canada "Governor of the Bank of Canada").[[36]](#cite_note-07-27-36) He immediately left his position at the Department of Finance to become an advisor to the outgoing governor, [David Dodge](/wiki/David_A._Dodge "David A. Dodge"), before formally assuming Dodge's position on February 1, 2008.[[36]](#cite_note-07-27-36) Carney was selected over [Paul Jenkins](/wiki/Paul_Jenkins_(economist) "Paul Jenkins (economist)"), the senior deputy governor, who had been considered the front-runner to succeed Dodge.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Carney took on this role at the beginning of the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"). At the time of his appointment, Carney was the youngest central bank governor among the [G8](/wiki/G8 "G8") and [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20") nations.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

#### 2008 financial crisis

Carney's actions as Governor of the Bank of Canada are said to have played a major role in helping Canada avoid the worst impacts of the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/G7_Finance_ministers.jpg/250px-G7_Finance_ministers.jpg)](/wiki/File:G7_Finance_ministers.jpg)

Carney, then [governor of the Bank of Canada](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_Canada "Governor of the Bank of Canada"), stands in the back row with other central bank governors during the 2008 [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") finance ministerial summit.

The epoch-making feature of Carney's tenure as governor remains the decision to cut the overnight rate by 50 [basis points](/wiki/Basis_point "Basis point") in March 2008, one month after his appointment. While the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") delivered a rate increase in July 2008, Carney anticipated the leveraged-loan crisis would trigger global [financial contagion](/wiki/Financial_contagion "Financial contagion"). When policy rates in Canada hit the effective lower bound, the central bank combated the crisis with the [non-standard monetary tool](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") "conditional commitment" in April 2009 to hold the policy rate for at least one year, in a boost to domestic credit conditions and market confidence. Output and employment began to recover from mid-2009, in part thanks to monetary stimulus.[[41]](#cite_note-VermaEuromoney12-41) The Canadian economy outperformed those of its G7 peers during the crisis, and Canada was the first G7 nation to have both its Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and employment recover to pre-crisis levels.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The Bank of Canada's decision to provide substantial additional [liquidity](/wiki/Liquidity "Liquidity") to the Canadian financial system,[[43]](#cite_note-43) and its unusual step of announcing a commitment to keep interest rates at their lowest possible level for one year,[[44]](#cite_note-44) appear to have been significant contributors to Canada's weathering of the crisis.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Mark_Carney_-_World_Economic_Forum_Annual_Meeting_2012.jpg/250px-Mark_Carney_-_World_Economic_Forum_Annual_Meeting_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_Carney_-_World_Economic_Forum_Annual_Meeting_2012.jpg)

Carney, then the governor of the [Bank of Canada](/wiki/Bank_of_Canada "Bank of Canada"), speaks at the 2012 [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum")'s "Beyond Basel: Financial Institution Regulation" panel".

The commitment to ultra-low lending rates led to a spike in housing prices and household debt.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In April 2012, Carney acknowledged there were "issues in some segments of the housing market" and some properties in Canada were "probably overvalued" but he was not overly concerned. He stated low-interest rates were not to blame but the onus was on individuals who take out the loans, the banks, and the federal government's mortgage lending rules.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Before Carney left for the Bank of England, there were calls to raise rates as Canadians were holding record-level debt and the housing market was overheated.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Canada's [risk-averse](/wiki/Risk-averse "Risk-averse") fiscal and regulatory environment is also cited as a factor. In 2009 a *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* columnist wrote, "Canada has done more than survive this financial crisis. The country is positively thriving in it. Canadian banks are well capitalized and poised to take advantage of opportunities that American and European banks cannot seize."[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Carney earned various accolades for his leadership during the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"): he was named one of *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*'s "Fifty who will frame the way forward"[[54]](#cite_note-54) and of *[Time Magazine](/wiki/Time_Magazine "Time Magazine")*'s *2010 Time 100*.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In May 2011, *[Reader's Digest](/wiki/Reader%27s_Digest "Reader's Digest")* named him "Editor's Choice for Most Trusted Canadian".[[10]](#cite_note-RD01-10) In October 2012, Carney was named "Central Bank Governor of the Year 2012" by the editors of *[Euromoney](/wiki/Euromoney "Euromoney")* magazine.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### International organization memberships

Carney was chairman of the [Bank for International Settlements](/wiki/Bank_for_International_Settlements "Bank for International Settlements")' [Committee on the Global Financial System](/wiki/Committee_on_the_Global_Financial_System "Committee on the Global Financial System") from July 2010 until January 2012.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Mark_Carney_World_Economic_Forum_2013_%283%29.jpg/250px-Mark_Carney_World_Economic_Forum_2013_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_Carney_World_Economic_Forum_2013_(3).jpg)

Carney speaks on the global economic outlook at the 2013 World Economic Forum panel.

Carney was a member of the [Group of Thirty](/wiki/Group_of_Thirty "Group of Thirty"), an international body of leading financiers and academics, and of the Foundation Board of the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum").[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) Carney attended the annual meetings of the [Bilderberg Group](/wiki/Bilderberg_Group "Bilderberg Group") in 2011, 2012 and 2019.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

On November 4, 2011, Carney was named chairman of the [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel")-based [Financial Stability Board](/wiki/Financial_Stability_Board "Financial Stability Board"), which coordinates international financial regulatory authorities.[[62]](#cite_note-62) In a statement, Carney credited his appointment to "the strong reputation of Canada's financial system and the leading role that Canada has played in helping to develop many of the most important international reforms".[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The three-year term was a part-time commitment, allowing Carney to complete his term at the Bank of Canada. While there had been no indication of his priorities as chairman, on the day of his appointment the Board published a list of 29 banks that were considered large enough to pose a risk to the global economy should they fail.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) At his first press conference as Chairman of the FSB in January 2012, Carney laid out his key priorities for the board.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In November 2014, Carney was reappointed to a second term as chairman.[[67]](#cite_note-67) This second term ended in 2018, while he was in his next post at the Bank of England.[[68]](#cite_note-:5-68)

### Governor of the Bank of England (2013–2020)

On November 26, 2012, Chancellor of the Exchequer [George Osborne](/wiki/George_Osborne "George Osborne") announced the appointment of Carney as [Governor of the Bank of England](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_England "Governor of the Bank of England").[[69]](#cite_note-69) He succeeded [Sir Mervyn King](/wiki/Sir_Mervyn_King "Sir Mervyn King") on July 1, 2013.[[70]](#cite_note-bbc-70) He was the first [Commonwealth citizen](/wiki/Commonwealth_citizen "Commonwealth citizen") from outside Britain to be appointed to the role since the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") was established in 1694. The Bank of England was given additional powers from 2013, such as the ability to set bank capital requirements.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Although the usual term for governor is eight years, Carney indicated that he only intended to serve for five years and stand down in 2018.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Reuni%C3%B3n_bilateral_-_Guido_Sandleris_y_Mark_Carney_%2844303351270%29.jpg/250px-Reuni%C3%B3n_bilateral_-_Guido_Sandleris_y_Mark_Carney_%2844303351270%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reuni%C3%B3n_bilateral_-_Guido_Sandleris_y_Mark_Carney_(44303351270).jpg)

Carney, then the [governor of the Bank of England](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Bank_of_England "Governor of the Bank of England"), attends a bilateral meeting with [Guido Sandleris](/wiki/Guido_Sandleris "Guido Sandleris"), the president of the [Central Bank of Argentina](/wiki/Central_Bank_of_Argentina "Central Bank of Argentina").

Before taking up the post, Carney had already indicated disagreement with the Bank of England's Executive Director of Financial Stability [Andy Haldane](/wiki/Andy_Haldane "Andy Haldane"), specifically on leverage ratios and bank break-ups. He has been quoted as saying that Haldane does not have a "proper understanding of the facts" on bank regulation.[[41]](#cite_note-VermaEuromoney12-41) He was thought to have been offered a total pay package of about [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")624,000 per year, approximately £100,000 more per year than his predecessor.[[70]](#cite_note-bbc-70)

Shortly before Carney took up the post, the Bank of England took up financial regulation duties from the [Financial Services Authority](/wiki/Financial_Services_Authority "Financial Services Authority"). Carney's changes to the Bank's operating procedures helped modernize the institution by making much more media appearances than predecessors, including controversial announcements during two referendums.[[68]](#cite_note-:5-68) Carney implemented a policy called "forward guidance" in which the Bank would not raise interest rates if unemployment was above 7% to try and encourage business lending.[[68]](#cite_note-:5-68) This policy, which contained many conditions, would later be considered confusing and complicated.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In May 2014, Carney warned the UK's heated housing market was the biggest risk to financial stability, and he was considering providing advice on the [Help to Buy](/wiki/Help_to_Buy "Help to Buy") mortgage scheme, which some believed was contributing to housing inflation.[[74]](#cite_note-auto-74) He stated UK housing prices and the lack of [affordability of housing in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Affordability_of_housing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Affordability of housing in the United Kingdom") was due to limited supply, and stated that twice as many homes were built in Canada than in the UK, although Canada had half the population.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Carney stated foreign cash buyers in the London market were beyond his control but he believed they did not pose a significant risk to the rest of the country. Carney assured the public the Bank was monitoring rising property prices and large-value mortgages to avoid [debt overhang](/wiki/Debt_overhang "Debt overhang") destabilizing the economy.[[74]](#cite_note-auto-74)

In 2014, Carney warned that if the [Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") was successful, the new country would likely not be able to continue using the [pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling") without ceding some powers to the UK.[[68]](#cite_note-:5-68)

In 2015, Carney changed the number of yearly interest rate meetings from twelve to eight and ordered minutes to be published during the announcements.[[68]](#cite_note-:5-68)

Before the [2016 Brexit referendum](/wiki/2016_Brexit_referendum "2016 Brexit referendum"), Carney warned that leaving the European Union could cause a recession. After the [resignation of Prime Minister David Cameron](/wiki/Resignation_of_David_Cameron "Resignation of David Cameron"), he made another public announcement shortly after the result supporting a departure, in which he announced that the financial system would operate normally to assuage public concerns. Afterwards, the bank cut interest rates in half from 0.5% to 0.25% and restarted [quantitative easing](/wiki/Quantitative_easing "Quantitative easing").[[68]](#cite_note-:5-68) At the urging of Prime Minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") and [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") [Philip Hammond](/wiki/Philip_Hammond "Philip Hammond"), Carney agreed to extend his term while the UK undertook [Brexit negotiations](/wiki/Brexit_negotiations "Brexit negotiations"), but only for one year.[[76]](#cite_note-76) He further agreed to an additional seven months in September 2018, to "support a smooth exit" from the EU,[[77]](#cite_note-77) and to a further two months for an orderly transition to his successor, [Andrew Bailey](/wiki/Andrew_Bailey_(banker) "Andrew Bailey (banker)").[[78]](#cite_note-78)

At the start of the [COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"), as Carney was set to leave the governorship in March 2020, the bank cut interest rates by 0.5% to protect against the pandemic's expected economic shocks.[[68]](#cite_note-:5-68)

### Post-governorships (2020–2024)

In 2020, Carney served as one of many informal advisors to Canadian prime minister [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau"), advising him on the government's [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") economic response.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-auto2-81) Carney reportedly advised Trudeau on [Canada's response](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Canada "COVID-19 pandemic in Canada") to the COVID-19 pandemic, with Trudeau looking to Carney to help Canada get out of its [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession").[[82]](#cite_note-82) Due to this, Carney was speculated to potentially become [Minister of Finance](/wiki/Minister_of_Finance_(Canada) "Minister of Finance (Canada)"), and later, Canadian prime minister if Trudeau resigned.[[81]](#cite_note-auto2-81)

In October 2020, Carney was vice chairman at [Brookfield Asset Management](/wiki/Brookfield_Asset_Management "Brookfield Asset Management") (BAM), where he led the firm's [environmental, social and governance (ESG)](/wiki/Environmental,_social_and_corporate_governance "Environmental, social and corporate governance") and impact fund investment strategy.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-:3-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) In February 2021, Carney retracted an earlier claim that the US$600 billion Brookfield Asset Management portfolio was [carbon neutral](/wiki/Carbon_neutral "Carbon neutral"). He had based his claim on the fact that Brookfield has a large [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") portfolio and "all the avoided emissions that come with that". The claim was criticized as accounting tricks because avoided emissions do not counteract the emissions from investments in coal and other fossil fuels responsible for Brookfield's [carbon footprint](/wiki/Carbon_footprint "Carbon footprint") of about 5,200 metric tons of carbon dioxide.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Ursula_von_der_Leyen_attends_the_2021_UN_Climate_Change_Conference_%2818%29.jpg/250px-Ursula_von_der_Leyen_attends_the_2021_UN_Climate_Change_Conference_%2818%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ursula_von_der_Leyen_attends_the_2021_UN_Climate_Change_Conference_(18).jpg)

Carney on a panel at the [COP26](/wiki/COP26 "COP26") alongside Canadian Prime Minister [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau"), managing director of the [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF") [Kristalina Georgieva](/wiki/Kristalina_Georgieva "Kristalina Georgieva"), [Director-General of the WTO](/wiki/Director-General_of_the_World_Trade_Organization "Director-General of the World Trade Organization") [Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala](/wiki/Ngozi_Okonjo-Iweala "Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala"), and [president of the European Commission](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Commission "President of the European Commission") [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen")

In 2020, Carney launched the [Taskforce on Scaling Voluntary Carbon Markets](/wiki/Taskforce_on_Scaling_Voluntary_Carbon_Markets "Taskforce on Scaling Voluntary Carbon Markets")—an initiative to increase trading of voluntary [carbon offsets](/wiki/Carbon_offset "Carbon offset")—[[89]](#cite_note-FT_20021203-89) with Bill Winters as Group Chief Executive.[[90]](#cite_note-TSVCM_2020-90) The TSVCM is sponsored by the [Institute of International Finance](/wiki/Institute_of_International_Finance "Institute of International Finance").[[90]](#cite_note-TSVCM_2020-90) Taskforce members include more than "40 leaders from six continents with backgrounds across the carbon market value chain", including representatives from the [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), [BlackRock](/wiki/BlackRock "BlackRock"), [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P.")'s New Energy Finance, [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas"), [BP](/wiki/BP "BP"), [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing"), [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), [Tata Steel](/wiki/Tata_Steel "Tata Steel"), [Total](/wiki/Total_SE "Total SE"), [IHS Markit](/wiki/IHS_Markit "IHS Markit"), and LSE.[[90]](#cite_note-TSVCM_2020-90) In a December 3, 2020 *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* article, Carney said that the voluntary global carbon offset market was an "imperative" to help reduce emissions. The *Times* article cited Carney saying London would likely host the "new pilot market for voluntary carbon offsets" that could be "set up" by December 2021.[[89]](#cite_note-FT_20021203-89)

In February 2021, Carney joined the board of fintech company [Stripe](/wiki/Stripe_(company) "Stripe (company)").[[91]](#cite_note-91) Carney helped launch the Glasgow Financial Alliance for Net Zero ([GFANZ](/wiki/GFANZ "GFANZ")) at COP26 in Glasgow in November 2021. He acts as the group's co-chair.[[92]](#cite_note-92) From 2022 to January 2025, Carney has also been an advisor to Watershed, a climate tech company founded by Stripe alumni that proposes to be "the platform companies need to succeed in the [climate economy](/wiki/Green_economy "Green economy")".[[93]](#cite_note-93) In August 2023, Carney was named by [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") as chair of the new board of directors for [Bloomberg L.P.](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P.") as part of a broader reshuffle of the company's leadership.[[94]](#cite_note-:4-94)

In September 2024, Carney became a special adviser and chair of the Liberal task force on economic growth.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) Shortly after the appointment, it was revealed Brookfield Asset Management had solicited the federal government for CA$10 billion in funds as part of $50 billion Canada-only asset fund. Carney did not need to follow standard ethical disclosures mandatory for prime ministerial advisors because he was employed by the Liberal Party rather than the Prime Minister's Office.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)

## Political beginnings

According to Carney, in 2012, Canadian Prime Minister [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") asked Carney—who was then governor of the Bank of Canada—if he would join the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") government as [minister of finance](/wiki/Minister_of_Finance_(Canada) "Minister of Finance (Canada)"). Carney declined, stating in a February 2025 interview with the [CBC](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") that he felt it "wasn't appropriate" for him to proceed with the offer because he felt it was not right to "go directly from being governor into elective politics."[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Carney was approached by some officials in the Liberal Party to run for leader in their [2013 leadership election](/wiki/2013_Liberal_Party_of_Canada_leadership_election "2013 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election"). He ultimately declined to do so.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Prime_Minister_Boris_Johnson_meets_with_Mark_Carney_No.10_Downing_Street.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_Boris_Johnson_meets_with_Mark_Carney_No.10_Downing_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_Boris_Johnson_meets_with_Mark_Carney_No.10_Downing_Street.jpg)

Carney meets with British Prime Minister Boris Johnson on January 16, 2020, to discuss COP26.

As he prepared to step down as governor of the Bank of England, Carney was appointed as United Nations special envoy for climate action and finance in March 2020.[[101]](#cite_note-101) In January 2020, UK Prime Minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") appointed Carney to the position of finance advisor for the UK presidency of the [COP26](/wiki/COP26 "COP26") [United Nations Climate Change conference](/wiki/United_Nations_Climate_Change_conference "United Nations Climate Change conference") in Glasgow.[[102]](#cite_note-102) At that time the conference was scheduled for November 2020, but it was later postponed to November 2021.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

In 2021, Carney spoke at the Liberal Party policy convention, declaring his support of the party but stopping short of pledging to run under its banner.[[104]](#cite_note-104) Later that year, he ruled himself out as a candidate in the then-speculated [2021 Canadian federal election](/wiki/2021_Canadian_federal_election "2021 Canadian federal election"), owing to his COP26 commitments.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

Carney endorsed [Catherine McKenney](/wiki/Catherine_McKenney "Catherine McKenney")'s candidacy for [mayor of Ottawa](/wiki/Mayor_of_Ottawa "Mayor of Ottawa") in the [2022 mayoral election](/wiki/2022_Ottawa_municipal_election "2022 Ottawa municipal election").[[106]](#cite_note-106)

In October 2023, he endorsed the UK [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)")'s [Shadow Chancellor](/wiki/Shadow_Chancellor "Shadow Chancellor") [Rachel Reeves](/wiki/Rachel_Reeves "Rachel Reeves") to be the next [chancellor of the exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_exchequer "Chancellor of the exchequer") in a video following Reeves' speech at the [Labour Party conference](/wiki/Labour_Party_conference "Labour Party conference") that year.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Following Labour's victory in the [2024 election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), Carney was part of a taskforce which saw the creation of a British [National Wealth Fund](/wiki/National_Wealth_Fund_(United_Kingdom)#2024:_National_Wealth_Fund "National Wealth Fund (United Kingdom)").[[108]](#cite_note-108)

On September 9, 2024, Carney was named by [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau") to chair the Liberal Party of Canada's leader's Task Force on Economic Growth.[[109]](#cite_note-109) His name was briefly mentioned upon the [resignation of Chrystia Freeland](/wiki/Resignation_of_Chrystia_Freeland "Resignation of Chrystia Freeland") as a possible candidate for [finance minister](/wiki/Minister_of_Finance_(Canada) "Minister of Finance (Canada)") in [Trudeau's ministry](/wiki/29th_Canadian_Ministry "29th Canadian Ministry").[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Leader of the Liberal Party

#### 2025 leadership election

Main article: [2025 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election](/wiki/2025_Liberal_Party_of_Canada_leadership_election "2025 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election")

On January 16, 2025, Carney officially announced that he was running in the [2025 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election](/wiki/2025_Liberal_Party_of_Canada_leadership_election "2025 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election").[[113]](#cite_note-BBC2025016-113) Carney also announced that he stepped down from all executive, board and advisory positions that he was part of to focus on his leadership campaign.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115) By February 9, his campaign had raised more than $1.9 million in donations from over 11,000 people and received endorsements from 66 Liberal caucus members.[[116]](#cite_note-116) On March 9, Carney won on the first ballot with over 85.9% of the vote, making him the leader of the Liberal Party.[[117]](#cite_note-electedliberalleader-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118) His margin of victory surpassed [Justin Trudeau's 2013 margin](/wiki/2013_Liberal_Party_of_Canada_leadership_election "2013 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election"), winning all 343 electoral districts.[[117]](#cite_note-electedliberalleader-117)

## Prime Minister of Canada (2025–present)

Main articles: [Premiership of Mark Carney](/wiki/Premiership_of_Mark_Carney "Premiership of Mark Carney") and [30th Canadian Ministry](/wiki/30th_Canadian_Ministry "30th Canadian Ministry")

### Swearing-in

On March 14, 2025, five days after winning the leadership election, Carney was sworn in as the 24th [prime minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Canada "Prime minister of Canada"), along with the [30th Canadian Ministry](/wiki/30th_Canadian_Ministry "30th Canadian Ministry").[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) Upon taking the oath of office, he became the first Canadian prime minister born in any of its territories (as opposed to provinces) and the third born west of Ontario (after [Joe Clark](/wiki/Joe_Clark "Joe Clark") and [Kim Campbell](/wiki/Kim_Campbell "Kim Campbell")). He is the second prime minister to have earned a PhD, after [William Lyon Mackenzie King](/wiki/William_Lyon_Mackenzie_King "William Lyon Mackenzie King"). Additionally, he is the first to have never served in prior elected office, and the first since [John Turner](/wiki/John_Turner "John Turner") not to be sitting in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") at the time of appointment.[[113]](#cite_note-BBC2025016-113)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

[![Mark Carney sits and talks with Prime Minister of the United Kingdom Keir Starmer flanked by Canadian and UK flags for each respective country's leader. Both are dressed in a dark suit, white shirt, and tie](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Starmer_and_Carney_2025-03-17-18-44.jpg/250px-Starmer_and_Carney_2025-03-17-18-44.jpg)](/wiki/File:Starmer_and_Carney_2025-03-17-18-44.jpg)

Carney meets with British Prime Minister [Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer") on March 17, 2025.

In his first act as prime minister, Carney signed a prime ministerial directive to end the [consumer carbon tax](/wiki/Carbon_pricing_in_Canada "Carbon pricing in Canada") by April 1, while ensuring that April's carbon rebate continues.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The directive was affirmed by an [order in council](/wiki/Order_in_council "Order in council") signed by Governor General [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon"). Carney's first foreign visits were to France and the United Kingdom on March 17 to strengthen mutual security and [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty"). During the latter visit, Carney met with King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") for the first time as prime minister.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

### 2025 federal election

Main article: [2025 Canadian federal election](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_election "2025 Canadian federal election")

Carney was widely expected to call a [federal parliamentary election](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_election "2025 Canadian federal election") for late April or early May 2025, ahead of the required election date in October. On March 22, the Liberal Party announced that Carney would contest the south Ottawa riding of [Nepean](/wiki/Nepean_(federal_electoral_district) "Nepean (federal electoral district)") in the election;[[125]](#cite_note-125) ridings in [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") had been floated given his personal connection to the province, particularly [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), as were safe Liberal seats in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") and [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa").[[126]](#cite_note-126) On March 23, Carney visited Governor General [Mary Simon](/wiki/Mary_Simon "Mary Simon") and asked to dissolve parliament and call an election for April 28.[[127]](#cite_note-127) Carney and the Liberal Party subsequently won the election, winning 169 seats and falling 3 seats short of a majority.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) Carney also won the Nepean riding, making him the first prime minister since [Wilfrid Laurier](/wiki/Wilfrid_Laurier "Wilfrid Laurier") in 1908–1910 to represent a riding in the Ottawa area and the first since [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") to represent a riding in [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario").[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Mark_Carney_%26_Charles_III_in_the_Senate_Chamber%2C_2025.jpg/250px-Mark_Carney_%26_Charles_III_in_the_Senate_Chamber%2C_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_Carney_%26_Charles_III_in_the_Senate_Chamber,_2025.jpg)

Carney and King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") in the Senate chamber during the [2025 Speech from the Throne](/wiki/2025_Speech_from_the_Throne "2025 Speech from the Throne")

To open the [45th Canadian Parliament](/wiki/45th_Canadian_Parliament "45th Canadian Parliament"), Carney invited Charles III to deliver the [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/2025_Speech_from_the_Throne "2025 Speech from the Throne"), an act described as a symbolic affirmation of Canada's sovereignty.[[132]](#cite_note-132) Among the items outlined in the speech was the government's intention to join [ReArm Europe](/wiki/ReArm_Europe "ReArm Europe"), a European-led defence initiative.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

### Domestic policy

Carney's government passed the first major legislation of his tenure, Bill C-5, the *[One Canadian Economy Act](/wiki/One_Canadian_Economy_Act "One Canadian Economy Act")* in June 2025 with Conservative support. The bill removes federal barriers to internal trade and expedites nation-building projects with Cabinet approval; it received opposition from some Indigenous advocacy groups.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137) His government presented the [2025 federal budget](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_budget "2025 Canadian federal budget") in November, promising to reduce the civil service by about 40,000 positions, cut new temporary resident admissions to 673,650 in 2025 to 385,000 in 2026,[[138]](#cite_note-138) balance operational spending by 2029, and reach the 2% of [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") target for military spending by 2026.[[139]](#cite_note-Policies-139) In addition, Carney reached a deal with [Danielle Smith](/wiki/Danielle_Smith "Danielle Smith") to build an [oil pipeline](/wiki/Pipelines_in_Canada "Pipelines in Canada") from Alberta to the [British Columbia Coast](/wiki/British_Columbia_Coast "British Columbia Coast"). Opposed by British Columbia's [David Eby](/wiki/David_Eby "David Eby"), most local First Nations, and some Liberal caucus members, the [memorandum of understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") (MOU) exempts the project from climate legislation such as the greenhouse gas emissions cap, but outlines that Alberta will invest in a carbon capture system for the [Athabasca oil sands](/wiki/Athabasca_oil_sands "Athabasca oil sands").[[140]](#cite_note-140) Following the signing of the MOU, [Steven Guilbeault](/wiki/Steven_Guilbeault "Steven Guilbeault"), former [minister of environment and climate change](/wiki/Minister_of_Environment_and_Climate_Change "Minister of Environment and Climate Change"), resigned from [Cabinet](/wiki/30th_Canadian_Ministry "30th Canadian Ministry") while remaining a member of the government caucus.[[141]](#cite_note-141) Between late 2025 and early 2026, multiple opposition MPs [crossed the floor](/wiki/Crossed_the_floor "Crossed the floor") to the Liberal caucus, helping push the government towards majority status.

### Foreign policy

In June 2025, Canada hosted the [51st G7 summit](/wiki/51st_G7_summit "51st G7 summit") in [Kananaskis, Alberta](/wiki/Kananaskis_Improvement_District "Kananaskis Improvement District"). In his capacity as chair, Carney opened the summit and invited other world leaders, including India's [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi") and Ukraine's [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy").[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144) In July 2025, Carney announced that at the next meeting of the [UN General Assembly](/wiki/UN_General_Assembly "UN General Assembly"), Canada would officially recognize a Palestinian state.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) In September 2025, Carney and Indonesian President [Prabowo Subianto](/wiki/Prabowo_Subianto "Prabowo Subianto") signed in Ottawa the first bilateral free trade agreement between Canada and an [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") member state.[[147]](#cite_note-147) In November 2025, Carney announced a $70 billion investment agreement with the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") (UAE) aimed at strengthening the Canadian economy.[[148]](#cite_note-148) However, the deal has drawn criticism from human rights advocates due to concerns over the UAE's involvement in arming the [Rapid Support Forces](/wiki/Rapid_Support_Forces "Rapid Support Forces") militia during the [Sudanese civil war](/wiki/Sudanese_civil_war_(2023%E2%80%93present) "Sudanese civil war (2023–present)").[[149]](#cite_note-149) Carney faced criticism after investigations linked Canadian-made weapons to [atrocities in Sudan](/wiki/War_crimes_during_the_Sudanese_civil_war_(2023%E2%80%93present) "War crimes during the Sudanese civil war (2023–present)").[[150]](#cite_note-150)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Prabowo-Mark_Carney_Sept_2025.jpg/250px-Prabowo-Mark_Carney_Sept_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prabowo-Mark_Carney_Sept_2025.jpg)

Carney and Indonesian President [Prabowo Subianto](/wiki/Prabowo_Subianto "Prabowo Subianto") in [Parliament Hill](/wiki/Parliament_Hill "Parliament Hill"), Ottawa, September 24, 2025

In January 2026, he made an official visit to [China](/wiki/China "China") and met with Chinese leader [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping"). The two agreed to lower tariffs on Canadian [canola oil](/wiki/Canola_oil "Canola oil") from 85% to 15% and on Chinese [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicles "Electric vehicles") from 100% to 6.1%.[[151]](#cite_note-Newsweek_Xi_Jinping-151)[[152]](#cite_note-major-152) During his official visit to Beijing, Carney announced the formation of a "new strategic partnership" with China that is "realistic, respectful and interest-based".[[152]](#cite_note-major-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153) On January 20, 2026, [Carney spoke](/wiki/2026_Mark_Carney_speech_at_the_World_Economic_Forum "2026 Mark Carney speech at the World Economic Forum") at the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/56th_World_Economic_Forum "56th World Economic Forum")'s annual meeting in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), where he declared that international economic partnerships were "in the midst of a rupture, not a transition", that the time of the [rules-based global order](/wiki/Rules-based_order "Rules-based order") was over, and that the [middle powers](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") must act together to avoid being victimized by American [hegemony](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony").[[154]](#cite_note-pm-20260120-154)[[155]](#cite_note-reuters-20260122-155) Carney received a [standing ovation](/wiki/Standing_ovation "Standing ovation") for his speech, unusual for World Economic Forum meetings.[[155]](#cite_note-reuters-20260122-155)

## Views

Carney has been described as moderate, centrist, and [technocratic](/wiki/Technocratic "Technocratic");[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158) he is ideologically characterized as a member of the [Blue Grits](/wiki/Blue_Grits "Blue Grits") faction of the [Liberal Party of Canada](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada"), being described as [economically liberal](/wiki/Economically_liberal "Economically liberal") and [socially liberal](/wiki/Socially_liberal "Socially liberal").[[159]](#cite_note-159)

During the 2023 Global Progress Action Summit, Carney advocated for progressives to build "health care, infrastructure, schools, opportunity, sustainability and prosperity".[[160]](#cite_note-160) Carney was noted for using "masters of our own house"—in French, *maîtres chez nous*—a phrase associated with the [Quiet Revolution](/wiki/Quiet_Revolution "Quiet Revolution").[[161]](#cite_note-161)

### Economics

#### Wealth inequality

In 2011, Carney referred to the [Occupy Wall Street](/wiki/Occupy_Wall_Street "Occupy Wall Street") protests as "entirely constructive", citing frustrations being felt "particularly in the United States" over inequality and increasing [CEO–worker pay gaps](/wiki/Wage_ratio "Wage ratio").[[162]](#cite_note-162) In December 2016, Carney warned of the societal risk of "staggering wealth [inequalities](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality")" in a Roscoe Lecture at [Liverpool John Moores University](/wiki/Liverpool_John_Moores_University "Liverpool John Moores University"): "The proportion of the wealth held by the richest 1% of Americans increased from 25% in 1990 to 40% in 2012 ... Globally, the share of wealth held by the richest 1% in the world rose from one-third in 2000 to one-half in 2010."[[163]](#cite_note-163)

#### Monetary policy

On August 23, 2019, Carney delivered a speech at the [Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Bank_of_Kansas_City "Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City")'s 2019 annual [Jackson Hole Economic Symposium](/wiki/Jackson_Hole_Economic_Symposium "Jackson Hole Economic Symposium") entitled "The Growing Challenges for Monetary Policy in the current International Monetary and Financial System". Carney said that the "widespread use of the US dollar"—the dominant currency pricing—"in trade invoicing, in place of the currency of either the producer or the importer",[[164]](#cite_note-voxeu_Brunnermeier_20190703-164) has had a "destablilizing" effect on the global economy.[[165]](#cite_note-reuters_20190823-165) About 50 per cent of international trade relies on the US dollar as the "currency of choice". This represents "five times greater than the US's share in world goods imports, and three times its share in world exports".[[166]](#cite_note-Carney_20190823-166) Dominant currency pricing is not problematic when there is "synchronized growth" globally, Carney said. When "the tide is rising in America while receding elsewhere", the system needs to be revamped. Carney cited an article[[164]](#cite_note-voxeu_Brunnermeier_20190703-164) by [Markus Brunnermeier](/wiki/Markus_Brunnermeier "Markus Brunnermeier"), [Harold James](/wiki/Harold_James_(historian) "Harold James (historian)"), and [Jean-Pierre Landau](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Landau "Jean-Pierre Landau") on the potential role of digital currency area (DCA) in redefining the international monetary system.[[166]](#cite_note-Carney_20190823-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Donald_Trump_greets_Prime_Minister_of_Canada_Mark_Carney%2C_Tuesday%2C_May_6%2C_2025%2C_at_the_West_Wing_entrance_of_the_White_House.jpg/250px-Donald_Trump_greets_Prime_Minister_of_Canada_Mark_Carney%2C_Tuesday%2C_May_6%2C_2025%2C_at_the_West_Wing_entrance_of_the_White_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Trump_greets_Prime_Minister_of_Canada_Mark_Carney,_Tuesday,_May_6,_2025,_at_the_West_Wing_entrance_of_the_White_House.jpg)

Carney meets with United States President Donald Trump on May 6, 2025.

Speaking just hours after US President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") posted a tweet blaming [Federal Reserve chairman](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_chairman "Federal Reserve chairman") [Jerome Powell](/wiki/Jerome_Powell "Jerome Powell")'s policies for creating fears of an economic recession and threatening China with more retaliatory tariffs, Carney urged central banks to collaborate in replacing the US dollar as [reserve currency](/wiki/Reserve_currency "Reserve currency"). He cautioned against choosing another new hegemonic reserve currency like the [renminbi](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") and suggested instead, a "new Synthetic Hegemonic Currency" (SHC), such as [Libra](/wiki/Libra_(cryptocurrency) "Libra (cryptocurrency)"),[[165]](#cite_note-reuters_20190823-165)[[166]](#cite_note-Carney_20190823-166)[[167]](#cite_note-Bloomberg_News_20190823-167) which could potentially be provided "through a network of central bank digital currencies", that would decrease the US dollar's "domineering influence" on trade worldwide.[[166]](#cite_note-Carney_20190823-166)[[167]](#cite_note-Bloomberg_News_20190823-167)

#### Moral to market sentiments

On December 2, 2020, Carney delivered the first of four [Reith Lectures](/wiki/Reith_Lectures "Reith Lectures")—the BBC's flagship annual series.[[168]](#cite_note-BBC_Carney_20201202-168) In "How We Get What We Value – From Moral to Market Sentiments", he said society had come to esteem financial value over human value and moved from market economies to market societies. The series covers a trio of crises: credit, Covid, and climate.[[168]](#cite_note-BBC_Carney_20201202-168)

#### Fiscal policy

After becoming prime minister, Carney reversed the Trudeau government's capital gains tax increase[[169]](#cite_note-169) and promised to implement a middle class tax cut and a new approach to federal budgeting that would save tax-payers money if appointed as prime minister.[[170]](#cite_note-:21-170)

#### Petroleum industry

In June 2025, after becoming prime minister, Carney voiced support for a new oil pipeline to the West Coast and a proposed $16.5 billion carbon capture system for the [Athabasca oil sands](/wiki/Athabasca_oil_sands "Athabasca oil sands").[[171]](#cite_note-171)[[172]](#cite_note-172) A memorandum of understanding with the [Government of Alberta](/wiki/Government_of_Alberta "Government of Alberta") to advance a new pipeline and carbon capture system was reached in November 2025.[[173]](#cite_note-173) The *One Canadian Economy Act* speeds up approval of projects deemed of national interest, potentially including mines and oil pipelines, and eliminates some trade barriers between provinces.[[174]](#cite_note-174)

### Foreign affairs

#### Brexit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Ursula_von_der_Leyen_and_Mark_Carney.jpg/250px-Ursula_von_der_Leyen_and_Mark_Carney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ursula_von_der_Leyen_and_Mark_Carney.jpg)

Carney with [European Commission](/wiki/Von_der_Leyen_Commission_II "Von der Leyen Commission II") President [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen") on May 17, 2025

Carney warned many times that [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") was expected to negatively influence the UK economy. Consequently, Brexit activists accused him of making statements favouring the UK's continued membership of the European Union (EU) before the [British EU-membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum").[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176) He replied that he felt it was his duty to speak up on such issues.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

In November 2018, Carney warned that large parts of the British economy were not ready for a [no-deal Brexit](/wiki/No-deal_Brexit "No-deal Brexit"). Speaking on BBC Radio 4's *[Today](/wiki/Today_(BBC_Radio_4) "Today (BBC Radio 4)")* program, Carney explained that fewer than half of businesses had initiated contingency plans.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

In February 2019, speaking about the global economy, Carney provided a less negative perspective on Brexit, stating that globalization has resulted in "imbalances of democracy and sovereignty", and that Brexit "is the first test of a new global order and could prove the acid test of whether a way can be found to broaden the benefits of openness while enhancing democratic accountability".[[179]](#cite_note-179)

Carney also said that the increase in the perception that a no-deal Brexit is likely is "evidenced by betting odds and financial market asset pricing", resulting in the UK now having "the highest FX (foreign exchange) implied volatility, the highest equity risk premium and lowest real yields of any advanced economy."[[166]](#cite_note-Carney_20190823-166)

#### NATO

In February 2025, Carney committed that the Canadian government would spend 2% of Canada's GDP on defence (the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") target) by the end of 2030.[[170]](#cite_note-:21-170) In June 2025, Carney committed that the government would reach the 2% target by March 2026 and NATO's new 5% of GDP target by 2035.[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[182]](#cite_note-182)

#### Iran–Israel conflict

Following the [Israeli attacks on Iran](/wiki/June_2025_Israeli_strikes_on_Iran "June 2025 Israeli strikes on Iran") in June 2025, Carney reaffirmed "Israel's right to defend itself" and called for restraint.[[183]](#cite_note-183) Carney supported the [2026 Israeli–United States strikes on Iran](/wiki/2026_Israeli%E2%80%93United_States_strikes_on_Iran "2026 Israeli–United States strikes on Iran") while stressing that Canada was not party to the operation.[[184]](#cite_note-184)

#### Gaza war

In April 2025, Carney disagreed with the accusation that Israel was committing [genocide in Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide"), but supported an arms embargo on Israel.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186) In June 2025, he compared the [Israeli invasion of the Gaza Strip](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Israeli invasion of the Gaza Strip") and [occupation of the West Bank](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") to the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[187]](#cite_note-jns.org-187) In September 2025, he announced Canada's [formal recognition](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Palestine "International recognition of Palestine") of the [State of Palestine](/wiki/State_of_Palestine "State of Palestine"), becoming the first [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") nation to do so.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

In October 2025, Carney confirmed that Canada would fulfill its commitments to the [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court") (ICC) and stated that, should Israeli Prime Minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") enter Canadian territory, he would be detained.[[189]](#cite_note-189)

#### India

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Prime_Minister_of_Bharat%2C_Shri_Narendra_Damodardas_Modi_with_the_Prime_Minister_of_Canada%2C_Mr._Mark_J_Carney.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_of_Bharat%2C_Shri_Narendra_Damodardas_Modi_with_the_Prime_Minister_of_Canada%2C_Mr._Mark_J_Carney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_of_Bharat,_Shri_Narendra_Damodardas_Modi_with_the_Prime_Minister_of_Canada,_Mr._Mark_J_Carney.jpg)

Carney with Indian Prime Minister [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi") at the [51st G7 summit](/wiki/51st_G7_summit "51st G7 summit") on June 17, 2025

Under Prime Minister Mark Carney, [Canada–India relations](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93India_relations "Canada–India relations") have entered a period of diplomatic reset following a [diplomatic row between the two countries](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93India_diplomatic_row "Canada–India diplomatic row") during [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau")'s tenure. Carney, who described it as a "great honor" to host Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi at the [2025 G7 summit](/wiki/51st_G7_summit "51st G7 summit"), has adopted a pragmatic foreign policy focused on economic cooperation and reducing Canada’s reliance on the United States. The summit marked a turning point, with both leaders emphasizing shared priorities such as clean energy, artificial intelligence, and counterterrorism. Modi called for "win-win cooperation," and India’s Ministry of External Affairs announced plans to resume high-level dialogues and revive stalled free trade negotiations. These developments signal a renewed commitment to strengthening bilateral ties across a broad range of sectors.[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191)

On June 23, 2025, Carney issued a statement marking the 40th anniversary of the [Air India Flight 182 bombing](/wiki/Air_India_Flight_182_bombing "Air India Flight 182 bombing") carried out by Canada-based terrorist group [Babbar Khalsa](/wiki/Babbar_Khalsa "Babbar Khalsa") a week after the [Air India Flight 171](/wiki/Air_India_Flight_171 "Air India Flight 171") crash occurred in [Ahmedabad](/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad"), describing it as the "deadliest attack in our country’s history." Referring to the 1985 tragedy, in which 329 people—most of them Canadians of Indian descent[[192]](#cite_note-192)—were killed in a terrorist bombing carried out by Canada-based extremists, Carney emphasized that "this terrorist attack remains the deadliest attack in our country’s history – one we must never forget." Observing the National Day of Remembrance for Victims of Terrorism, he reaffirmed that Canada’s new government "unequivocally" stands against terrorism and paid tribute to the victims of the Kanishka bombing.[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194)

#### China

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/20251101_Sanae_Takaichi_at_the_APEC_South_Korea_2025_%285%29.jpg/250px-20251101_Sanae_Takaichi_at_the_APEC_South_Korea_2025_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20251101_Sanae_Takaichi_at_the_APEC_South_Korea_2025_(5).jpg)

Carney with [Japanese Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan") [Sanae Takaichi](/wiki/Sanae_Takaichi "Sanae Takaichi") at [APEC South Korea 2025](/wiki/APEC_South_Korea_2025 "APEC South Korea 2025")

On June 9, 2025, in his speech on defence spending, Carney warned of new threats to Canada's security and sovereignty, including those coming from an "assertive China."[[187]](#cite_note-jns.org-187) However, relations improved after Carney became Prime Minister, particularly after he held a meeting with Chinese leader [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") in October 2025 at the sidelines of the [APEC summit in South Korea](/wiki/APEC_South_Korea_2025 "APEC South Korea 2025"); both countries termed the meeting as a "turning point". The improvement in the relationship happened shortly after the deterioration of [Canada's relationship with the United States](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Canada–United States relations") under [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump").[[195]](#cite_note-:02-195) Carney visited China for three days in mid-January 2026, securing a preliminary agreement-in-principle.[[196]](#cite_note-196) Canada intends to lower tariffs on Chinese EVs from 100% to 6.1%, and China is expected to lower tariffs on Canadian canola oil and other goods. Carney stated, “I believe the progress we have made, and the partnership, sets us up well for the new world order.” When asked about [human rights in China](/wiki/Human_rights_in_China "Human rights in China"), Carney responded, “We take the world as it is, not as we wish it to be.”[[151]](#cite_note-Newsweek_Xi_Jinping-151)[[197]](#cite_note-197)

#### Trade

See also: [2025 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico](/wiki/2025_United_States_trade_war_with_Canada_and_Mexico "2025 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico") and [Canada–China trade war](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93China_trade_war "Canada–China trade war")

During the 2025 Liberal Party Leadership Race, and in light of aggressive [actions](/wiki/2025_United_States_trade_war_with_Canada_and_Mexico "2025 United States trade war with Canada and Mexico") taken by the United States under the [second Trump administration](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump"), Carney said that he supported deepening relationships with fellow [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") member states the United Kingdom, Australia, and New Zealand.[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199)

### Environmentalism

Carney discussing a net zero economy

Carney has often advocated for environmental sustainability.[[113]](#cite_note-BBC2025016-113) He was appointed as a [United Nations Special Envoy on Climate Change](/wiki/United_Nations_Special_Envoy_on_Climate_Change "United Nations Special Envoy on Climate Change") in 2019.[[200]](#cite_note-:6-200)[[201]](#cite_note-201)[[202]](#cite_note-202) In March 2021, Carney stated that in Canada there is a "huge economic opportunity" in the transition to a sustainable economy with reduced carbon emissions. He says that it is time to listen to scientists that have long been warning about the risks posed by climate change.[[200]](#cite_note-:6-200) Carney had initially supported the consumer carbon tax as implemented in 2019, but stated in a May 2024 [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") committee hearing that the tax had "served a purpose up until now."[[203]](#cite_note-203)[[204]](#cite_note-204) In 2020, he called the [green transition](/wiki/Green_transition "Green transition") "the greatest commercial opportunity of our time".[[205]](#cite_note-205)

In a January 2025 interview with [Jon Stewart](/wiki/Jon_Stewart "Jon Stewart"), Carney attributed much of Canada's [emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") to the [oil industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry_in_Canada "Petroleum industry in Canada"), which he argued must become cleaner rather than ordinary Canadians changing their lifestyles.[[206]](#cite_note-206) During his Liberal leadership campaign, Carney proposed replacing the existing consumer carbon tax with an incentive program to reward green choices, while keeping tax on large industrial emitters.[[207]](#cite_note-:19-207) He also promised to introduce a "carbon border-adjustment" to penalize high-polluting foreign imports.[[207]](#cite_note-:19-207) Carney supported Trudeau government's [EV mandate](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicles_in_Canada "Plug-in electric vehicles in Canada"),[[208]](#cite_note-208) requiring hybrids and [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") to make up 20% of sales by 2026 and 100% by 2035.[[209]](#cite_note-209)

In government, Carney eliminated the federal carbon tax, suspended the federal fossil fuels emissions cap, and came to a [Memorandum of Understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") with Alberta that did not rule out a new oil pipeline to the west coast, despite the objections of British Columbia and Indigenous leaders, leading to his criticism from environmentalists (see [§ 45th Parliament](#45th_Parliament)).[[210]](#cite_note-:1-210) Though Green Party leader [Elizabeth May](/wiki/Elizabeth_May "Elizabeth May") supported the [November Budget](/wiki/2025_Canadian_federal_budget "2025 Canadian federal budget"), she later admitted, in the wake of the Alberta MOU that it was a mistake. May called it a "significant betrayal and a reversal" and doubted the Prime Minister's faithfulness to his word.[[211]](#cite_note-211) In December 2025, Carney criticized the Trudeau government's approach to environmental protection as "too much regulation, not enough action".[[210]](#cite_note-:1-210)

### Immigration

In 2025, Carney campaigned to address "unsustainable" [immigration to Canada](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada"),[[212]](#cite_note-theglobeandmail-immigration-212) which had risen to approximately 500,000 a year during Justin Trudeau's premiership.[[213]](#cite_note-213)[[214]](#cite_note-214) The Liberal Party pledged to reduce the number of permanent residents admitted to Canada to less than 1% of the Canadian population per year, or less than 410,000 by 2025.[[215]](#cite_note-immigrationnewscanada.ca-2025-215) As per the Immigration Levels Plan 2025–2027, Canada's permanent resident admission targets are 395,000 in 2025, 380,000 in 2026, and 365,000 in 2027.[[215]](#cite_note-immigrationnewscanada.ca-2025-215) The government set levels for temporary migrants in the 2025–2027 Levels Plan at 673,650 in 2025, 516,600 in 2026, and 543,600 in 2027.[[212]](#cite_note-theglobeandmail-immigration-212)[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217)

In March 2025, Carney promised to reduce the number of temporary migrants and international students to less than 5% of the Canadian population by the end of 2027, saying" "It's a sharp drop from the recent high of 7.3 per cent. This will help ease strains on housing, on public infrastructure and social services."[[212]](#cite_note-theglobeandmail-immigration-212)

### Housing

In a March 2024 op-ed in *[The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail")*, Carney advocated for a low-carbon housing policy favouring [densification](/wiki/Densification "Densification") over [sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"). He argued that it should be easier for a homebuilder to densify by eliminating unit maxima, abolishing [parking minima](/wiki/Parking_mandates "Parking mandates") and allowing taller buildings and more density near transit lines.[[218]](#cite_note-218)

## Personal life

[![Mark Carney stands in front of a vertical banner for the 2018 G20 Summit with his wife, Diana Carney. He wears a dark blazer, pants, and a grey shirt, while she wears a dark jacket, shirt, and pants.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Llegada_de_Mark_Carney%2C_presidente_del_Consejo_de_Estabilidad_Financiera_%2831162633647%29.jpg/250px-Llegada_de_Mark_Carney%2C_presidente_del_Consejo_de_Estabilidad_Financiera_%2831162633647%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Llegada_de_Mark_Carney,_presidente_del_Consejo_de_Estabilidad_Financiera_(31162633647).jpg)

Carney and his wife [Diana Fox Carney](/wiki/Diana_Fox_Carney "Diana Fox Carney") at the [2018 G20 Buenos Aires summit](/wiki/2018_G20_Buenos_Aires_summit "2018 G20 Buenos Aires summit")

Carney met British economist [Diana Fox](/wiki/Diana_Fox "Diana Fox"), who is also active in various environmental and [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") causes,[[219]](#cite_note-219) while studying at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford").[[1]](#cite_note-auto1-1)[[10]](#cite_note-RD01-10) They were married in July 1994 while he was finishing his doctoral thesis.[[220]](#cite_note-220) They have four children; the couple lived in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") before moving to the [Rockcliffe Park](/wiki/Rockcliffe_Park "Rockcliffe Park") neighbourhood of Ottawa and then moving to London in 2013.[[6]](#cite_note-Scoffield-6) They moved back to Ottawa when Carney left his role at the Bank of England in 2020.[[221]](#cite_note-221)

Carney's younger brother Brian lives in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"). His brother-in-law is [the 3rd Baron Rotherwick](/wiki/Robin_Cayzer,_3rd_Baron_Rotherwick "Robin Cayzer, 3rd Baron Rotherwick"), to whom his wife's sister is married. He is also godfather to the son of fellow politician [Chrystia Freeland](/wiki/Chrystia_Freeland "Chrystia Freeland"), who ran against him in the [2025 Liberal Party leadership election](/wiki/2025_Liberal_Party_of_Canada_leadership_election "2025 Liberal Party of Canada leadership election").[[222]](#cite_note-222)

Carney is a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"),[[223]](#cite_note-223) and was named the most influential Catholic in Britain by *[The Tablet](/wiki/The_Tablet "The Tablet")* in 2015.[[224]](#cite_note-224)[[225]](#cite_note-225) He speaks French[[226]](#cite_note-226)[[227]](#cite_note-227)[[228]](#cite_note-228) but has described his proficiency as "far from perfect".[[229]](#cite_note-229) He was a [triple citizen](/wiki/Multiple_citizenship "Multiple citizenship") of Canada, Ireland and the United Kingdom, having obtained Irish citizenship in the late 1980s,[[230]](#cite_note-230) and British citizenship in 2018 – the latter while governor of the Bank of England, fulfilling a commitment he made when accepting the role.[[231]](#cite_note-231)[[232]](#cite_note-232) Shortly before becoming Canadian Prime Minister in March 2025, he said that he had started the process of formally renouncing his British and Irish citizenship to signify his commitment to Canada.[[233]](#cite_note-233) In April 2025, his campaign stated that he had renounced the other citizenships prior to being sworn in as prime minister.[[234]](#cite_note-234)

Carney has distant relatives in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") and is a supporter of the city's [soccer](/wiki/Soccer "Soccer") team [Everton FC](/wiki/Everton_FC "Everton FC"), though his wife supports [Arsenal FC](/wiki/Arsenal_FC "Arsenal FC").[[235]](#cite_note-235) He is also a supporter of [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers") and [football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") team [Edmonton Elks](/wiki/Edmonton_Elks "Edmonton Elks").[[236]](#cite_note-236) He completed the 2015 [London Marathon](/wiki/London_Marathon "London Marathon") in 3 hours, 31 minutes, and 22 seconds, marking a 17-minute improvement on his time at the 2011 [Ottawa Marathon](/wiki/Ottawa_Marathon "Ottawa Marathon").[[237]](#cite_note-237)[[238]](#cite_note-238)

## Published works

The title of Carney's [DPhil](/wiki/DPhil "DPhil") thesis is *The Dynamic Advantage of Competition*.[[239]](#cite_note-239)

Carney's first book, *[Value(s): Building a Better World for All](/wiki/Value(s):_Building_a_Better_World_for_All "Value(s): Building a Better World for All")*, was published by Signal Books in 2021.[[240]](#cite_note-240) His second book, *The Hinge: Time to Build an Even Better Canada*, was indefinitely delayed from its scheduled publishing date of May 13, 2025.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

## Honours and distinctions

* Officer of the [Order of Canada](/wiki/Order_of_Canada "Order of Canada") (OC) on November 20, 2014.[[242]](#cite_note-242)
* [Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Diamond_Jubilee_Medal "Queen Elizabeth II Diamond Jubilee Medal") in 2012.[[243]](#cite_note-243)
* [Freeman of the City of London](/wiki/Freeman_of_the_City_of_London "Freeman of the City of London").[[244]](#cite_note-244)
* Honorary degree of [Doctor of Laws](/wiki/Doctor_of_Laws "Doctor of Laws") (LL.D) from the [University of Manitoba](/wiki/University_of_Manitoba "University of Manitoba") on April 5, 2013.[[245]](#cite_note-245)
* Honorary degree of [Doctor of Laws](/wiki/Doctor_of_Laws "Doctor of Laws") (LL.D) from the [University of Alberta](/wiki/University_of_Alberta "University of Alberta") in Spring 2016.[[246]](#cite_note-246)
* Honorary degree of [Doctor of Laws](/wiki/Doctor_of_Laws "Doctor of Laws") (LL.D) from the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto") on June 18, 2018.[[247]](#cite_note-247)
* Honorary degree from the [London Business School](/wiki/London_Business_School "London Business School") on July 16, 2019.[[248]](#cite_note-248)
* [National Business Book Award](/wiki/National_Business_Book_Award "National Business Book Award") (2021)[[249]](#cite_note-249)
